sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE, SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 42, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'DOWN': 'Moves blue player 4 rows down, decreases timer by 2 pixels; stopped at row 44', 'LEFT': 'Moves blue player 4 cols left, decreases timer by 2 pixels', 'UP': 'Moves blue player 4 rows up, decreases timer by 2 pixels; stops at row 12', 'RIGHT': 'Moves blue player 4 cols right, decreases timer by 2 pixels', 'SPACE': 'Decreases timer by 2, no movement or other effect VERIFIED(step 37)', 'MOUSE': 'Decreases timer by 2, no special effect on blocks or containers VERIFIED(step 33-36)'}
- Object taxonomy: {'N_strip_top': 'Green timer bar at row 0, starts at 64 pixels', 'O_background': 'Orange main background', 'c_block': 'Charcoal block at rows 1-3, cols 36-39 (3x4)', 'M_block': 'Magenta block at rows 4-7, cols 36-39 (4x4)', 'b_rectangle': 'Blue 4x20 player rectangle, moves with arrow keys', 'Y_shapes': 'Two U-shaped yellow containers at bottom rows 52-59, left at cols 16-27, right at cols 40-51', 'w_strip_bottom': 'Light gray floor rows 60-63'}
- HUD map: {'top_green_strip': 'Timer - decreases 2 per action, game over at 0'}
- Win pattern: {'game_over': 'Timer reaches 0 causes game over and reset VERIFIED(step 32)'}
- Level log: {'level_1_attempt_1': 'Failed - timer ran out after 31 actions exploring movement'}
Current level:
- World model: Player at rows 16-19, cols 20-39. Timer at 53. All interactions tried so far just decrease timer.
- Goal model: Unknown - need to find what actually changes game state. Yellow containers look like targets, blocks look like objects to move.
- Recent findings: ['All actions (arrows, SPACE, MOUSE) decrease timer by 2', 'Player movement bounded: rows 12-44', 'Game resets after game over']
- Open questions: ['What is the goal?', 'Can blocks be moved or dropped?', 'Do yellow containers receive anything?']
- Plan: Try moving player to center above left container and see if that matters
- Strategy log: ['Tried MOUSE on blocks and containers: no effect beyond timer decrease']
- Failed probes: ['MOUSE on charcoal block at row 2 col 37: no effect (step 33)', 'MOUSE on yellow container at row 55 col 20: no effect (step 36)', 'Overlapping player with blocks: no effect (step 34)', 'SPACE under blocks: no effect (step 37)']
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 41 actions; a strong score needs about 39 or fewer. You are 1.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Timer is at ~43 now. I have about 20 more actions before game over. Let me think about what I haven't tried yet.
I've tried:
1. Moving player in all directions
2. MOUSE on blocks, containers, player
3. SPACE in various positions
4. Overlapping player with blocks
What I haven't tried:
1. Moving the player to the TOP position (row 12) and then doing something
2. Moving the player to the BOTTOM position (row 44) and then doing something
3. Moving the player to be directly above a container's opening
4. Some combination of movement + SPACE
Wait - let me reconsider the game. The blocks are at rows 1-7, cols 36-39. The containers are at rows 52-59. What if the goal is to make the blocks fall into the containers? But how?
What if the player needs to "push" the blocks? But the player is below the blocks, and the blocks don't move when the player overlaps them.
What if I need to move the player to the TOP (row 12) and then push the blocks DOWN? The blocks are at rows 1-7, and the player can go to row 12. There's a gap between them.
Actually, wait - what if the player needs to be at a specific row to interact with the blocks? Let me try moving UP to row 12 (the highest the player can go) and then pressing SPACE or moving RIGHT into the blocks.
Actually, I just realized something. The blocks are at cols 36-39, and the player is 20 columns wide. When the player is at cols 16-35, the blocks are just to the right. When the player moves RIGHT, it goes to cols 20-39, overlapping with the blocks. But what if the player needs to be at a specific vertical position to push the blocks?
Let me try: move UP to row 12, then move RIGHT so the player is at cols 20-39 (overlapping with blocks horizontally), then try SPACE or another action.
But wait, I already tried overlapping with blocks and it didn't work. The player was at rows 16-19 when that happened. What if the player needs to be closer to the blocks vertically?
Let me try: UP UP (to get to row 12), then RIGHT (to overlap with blocks), then SPACE.
Actually, I should be more strategic. Let me think about what the game could be:
- It looks like a "drop blocks into containers" game
- The blocks need to fall from rows 1-7 down to rows 52-59
- The player might need to "release" or "drop" the blocks somehow
What if SPACE is the "drop" action, but it only works when the player is touching the blocks? The player can't reach the blocks vertically (player min row is 12, blocks max row is 7). But what if the player can still "interact" with blocks that are in the same column range?
Let me try: move UP to row 12, move RIGHT to overlap with blocks, then SPACE.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Timer ~43, still have ~20 actions
# New hypothesis: player needs to be as close as possible to blocks vertically
# Try: UP to row 12, RIGHT to overlap blocks, then SPACE
seg = current_frame.segmentation
player = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color']=='b'][0]
print(f"Player: {player['boundary']}")
timer = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color']=='N'][0]['pixels']
print(f"Timer: {timer}")

# Move UP twice (to row 12), RIGHT to overlap blocks, then SPACE
action(['UP', 'UP', 'RIGHT', 'SPACE'])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"DOWN": "Moves blue player 4 rows down, decreases timer by 2", "LEFT": "Moves blue player 4 cols left, decreases timer by 2", "UP": "Moves blue player 4 rows up, decreases timer by 2; stops at row 12", "RIGHT": "Moves blue player 4 cols right, decreases timer by 2", "SPACE": "Decreases timer by 2, no movement or other effect", "MOUSE": "Decreases timer by 2, no special effect on any object"}, "object_taxonomy": {"N_strip_top": "Green timer bar at row 0", "O_background": "Orange main background", "c_block": "Charcoal block at rows 1-3, cols 36-39 (3x4)", "M_block": "Magenta block at rows 4-7, cols 36-39 (4x4)", "b_rectangle": "Blue 4x20 player rectangle", "Y_shapes": "Two U-shaped yellow containers at bottom", "w_strip_bottom": "Light gray floor"}, "hud_map": {"top_green_strip": "Timer - decreases 2 per action"}, "win_pattern": {"game_over": "Timer reaches 0 causes game over"}, "level_log": {"level_1_attempt_1": "Failed - timer ran out after 31 actions", "level_1_attempt_2": "41 actions, all probes failed - trying new approach after reset"}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Player at rows 16-19, cols 16-35. Timer at ~43.", "goal_model": "Hypothesis: need to be close to blocks vertically to interact with them", "recent_findings": [], "open_questions": ["Does being closer to blocks vertically enable interaction?"], "current_plan": "Move UP to row 12, RIGHT to overlap blocks, SPACE to drop them", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["MOUSE on charcoal block", "MOUSE on yellow container", "MOUSE on player", "Overlapping blocks at row 16-19", "SPACE under blocks at row 16-19", "SPACE aligned with container"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
